Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe glance at stuff when I felt like it, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because my mind was racing with anxiety. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't bring myself to care enough to set up a proper schedule. Most of my studying happened in random bursts when something caught my attention visually, like a good diagram or video.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Sometimes I'd go days without looking at anything, then I'd have a night where my anxiety kept me up and I'd watch a bunch of lecture videos in one sitting. Deadlines didn't really motivate me the way they probably should have - I'd often let assignments slide even when I knew they were due. It's weird because I'd still absorb the content, just not do the actual work.
Q3: Usually boredom, if I'm being honest. I'd be scrolling through my phone, nothing interesting happening, and I'd think 'might as well check the LMS.' Sometimes my anxiety would spike and I'd log in just to make sure I wasn't missing something catastrophic. Occasionally a notification would remind me something existed, but that wasn't really a strong motivator for me.
Q4: I guess watching videos became somewhat routine because it was the easiest thing to do. I could just sit there and let information wash over me without having to actively do anything. It happened naturally because videos are visual and don't require me to write or submit anything. But I wouldn't say I ever felt genuinely committed to logging in regularly - it was more like a default activity when I had nothing better to do.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me. I'm a visual learner, so seeing concepts explained with diagrams and animations actually stuck in my brain. I watched over 230 minutes of videos because that format just works for how my mind processes information. Reading walls of text or doing written exercises felt like pulling teeth in comparison.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the assignments - my completion rate was terrible, I know. Discussion forums felt pointless to me; I'd read what others wrote but rarely felt motivated to contribute anything myself. The written materials I'd skim at best. It's not that I couldn't do them, I just... couldn't make myself care enough. The anxiety made starting things feel overwhelming, and my low motivation meant I'd just avoid them entirely.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if it was visually engaging and didn't require me to produce anything. Watching a well-made video explaining a concept? Sure, I'm in. Having to write a response or complete a worksheet? My brain would immediately check out. I also valued things that I could do passively - anything that let me absorb information without the pressure of performance.
Q8: Required activities actually stressed me out more, which made me avoid them even harder. The pressure of knowing something was mandatory triggered my anxiety, and then I'd procrastinate until it was too late. Optional stuff felt less threatening, so ironically I might engage with it more casually. But honestly, neither type really motivated me much - I approached both with the same general apathy.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual learning happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then review them on my tablet when I was lying in bed or on the bus. I also used YouTube to find alternative explanations of concepts when the course material felt dry. My family has good resources, so I had access to textbooks and a decent setup at home for studying offline.
Q10: Most of my exam preparation happened completely offline. I'd print out diagrams and stick them on my wall, make visual mind maps on paper, and quiz myself using flashcards I made by hand. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also did a lot of passive reviewing - just thinking through concepts while doing other things, which obviously doesn't register as 'activity.'
Q11: Constantly. I'd open a video once, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times on my own device. Same with PDFs and slides - one access in the LMS, but I'd study from the downloaded version for hours. It was just more convenient and didn't require me to stay logged in. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than my access logs would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety got really bad and I'd just shut down completely. I'd feel this overwhelming dread about everything I wasn't doing, which paradoxically made me do even less. Sometimes I'd go through phases of just feeling completely bored and disconnected from the whole course. My resilience isn't great, so small setbacks would knock me off track for days.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When it spiked, I'd either compulsively check the platform or avoid it entirely - there was no middle ground. Fatigue from not sleeping well made it hard to focus on anything requiring effort. I had extracurricular activities that sometimes gave me an excuse to not engage, even though I probably could have managed both if I'd tried harder.
Q14: All the time. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through content, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos without really processing them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd click through materials rapidly just to mark them as 'viewed' without actually engaging. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: Assignments were my biggest avoidance area. I knew they were important, but the thought of starting them triggered this wave of anxiety that made me shut down. I'd tell myself I'd do them later, then later would never come. It's frustrating because I understood the material well enough to ace exams, but the act of producing work felt insurmountable. My low motivation made it easy to justify postponing everything.
Q16: Deadlines should have motivated me, but they mostly just increased my anxiety without actually making me more productive. I'd watch the deadline approach, feel increasingly stressed, and then either rush something mediocre or just not submit at all. The pressure didn't translate into action for me - it just made me feel worse about myself while still not doing the work.
Q17: When I actually submitted something and got feedback, it did make me briefly more engaged. Positive feedback felt good and made me think 'maybe I should try more.' But that feeling never lasted long - my baseline apathy would return within a day or two. Negative feedback just confirmed my anxiety that I wasn't good enough, which made me want to engage even less.
Q18: The course structure was fine, I guess. Having videos available meant I could engage in my preferred way. But the emphasis on assignments for completion metrics didn't work for my style at all. I would have done better in a course that weighted exams more heavily, since that's where I actually showed what I knew. The structure assumed consistent effort, which just isn't how I operate.
Q19: The video content being well-produced made engagement easier - visual stuff always draws me in. Having materials available for download was crucial for how I actually studied. What made it harder was the assignment-heavy structure and the discussion forum requirements. Those felt like busywork that didn't match how I learn. Also, the pacing assumed steady engagement, but I work in irregular bursts.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd log in frequently, watch tons of videos, browse around, but then not submit the actual assignments. I think it happens when someone is absorbing content but struggling with the production side - either from anxiety, lack of motivation, or just not seeing the point of the assignments. You can be learning a lot without having anything tangible to show for it.
Q21: That also describes me, weirdly. My exam score was really high even though my assignment completion was terrible. I think some people just learn effectively through methods that don't show up in activity logs. If you're studying offline, using downloaded materials, or just naturally good at absorbing and retaining information, your LMS activity won't reflect your actual understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often someone accesses the platform, which videos they watch, what they click on. But they completely miss offline study, the quality of engagement during access, and whether someone actually understood anything. They also can't capture mental state - whether someone was anxiously scrolling or genuinely focused. The numbers tell you what happened, not what it meant.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, even if I'm not jumping through all the hoops. It's about intellectual connection with the content, not just completing tasks. I felt engaged when I was genuinely interested in a concept and wanted to understand it deeply. I didn't feel engaged when I was just going through motions to satisfy requirements.
Q24: My anxiety levels and how they affected everything wouldn't show up at all. The fact that I was actually learning and retaining information despite my terrible assignment record is invisible in the data. My offline studying, my visual learning strategies, my genuine interest in certain topics - none of that appears. The LMS sees a disengaged student who barely submits work, not someone who aced the exam because they actually understood the material.
